title: Aboriginal Songlines
summary: Australia's First Peoples develop a navigational and cosmological system encoded in song.
body: Australia's First Peoples develop a navigational and cosmological system encoded in song that maps the continent's entire landscape. Some songlines remain accurate to geological features formed 10,000+ years ago. The oldest continuous knowledge system on Earth.

title: First Nations Terra Management
summary: Indigenous peoples across the Americas practice sophisticated land management at continental scale.
body: Indigenous peoples across the Americas practice sophisticated land management — controlled burns, aquaculture, food forests — maintaining biodiversity at a scale that post-colonial 'wilderness' destroyed. Called 'wilderness' by colonizers who erased its management.

title: Polynesian Navigation
summary: Pacific Islanders navigate 10 million square miles of open ocean using only stars, waves, and birds.
body: Pacific Islanders navigate 10 million square miles of open ocean using only stars, wave patterns, birds, and phosphorescence. They reach Hawaii 1,500 years before Europeans know the Pacific exists. The greatest navigational achievement in pre-modern history.

title: Indigenous Genocide Begins
summary: Columbus's arrival begins the systematic destruction of the Western Hemisphere's civilizations.
body: Columbus's arrival in the Caribbean initiates the systematic destruction of the Western Hemisphere's civilizations. 90% of indigenous populations — an estimated 55 million people — die within 150 years from disease, enslavement, and slaughter. The largest demographic catastrophe in human history.

title: Trail of Tears
summary: US Indian Removal Act forces 60,000+ Native Americans from their ancestral lands. 15,000 die.
body: US Indian Removal Act forces 60,000+ Native Americans from their ancestral lands. 15,000 die on forced marches. Entire nations — Cherokee, Muscogee, Seminole, Choctaw, Chickasaw — are ethnically cleansed from the American Southeast.

title: Olmec Civilization
summary: Mesoamerica's 'mother culture' builds colossal stone heads, develops writing, and invents the concept of zero.
body: The Olmec, considered the 'mother culture' of Mesoamerica, flourish in the tropical lowlands of what is now southern Mexico. They carve colossal basalt heads weighing up to 50 tons, develop early forms of writing and calendrical systems, and likely invent the concept of zero — centuries before India. Their art, religion, and ballgame tradition influence every subsequent Mesoamerican civilization including the Maya and Aztec.

title: Haudenosaunee Confederacy
summary: The Iroquois Great Law of Peace — the oldest living participatory democracy. It influences the US Constitution.
body: The Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) form a confederacy of five (later six) nations under the Great Law of Peace — an oral constitution establishing representative governance, separation of powers, and women's political authority. Clan mothers choose and can depose chiefs. Benjamin Franklin and other founders study the confederacy's structure; the US Congress formally acknowledges its influence on the Constitution in 1988.

title: Great Zimbabwe
summary: A stone city of 18,000 people in southern Africa. European colonizers refuse to believe Africans built it.
body: The Kingdom of Zimbabwe builds a massive stone city without mortar in what is now southeastern Zimbabwe. At its peak around 1300 CE, Great Zimbabwe houses 18,000 people and controls gold trade between the interior and the Swahili coast. When European colonizers encounter the ruins, they attribute them to Phoenicians, Arabs, or Biblical figures — refusing for over a century to accept that Africans built one of the medieval world's great cities.

title: Wounded Knee Occupation
summary: AIM occupies Wounded Knee for 71 days. The Indigenous rights movement enters the global stage.
body: Members of the American Indian Movement (AIM) occupy the town of Wounded Knee on the Pine Ridge Reservation for 71 days, demanding treaty rights and sovereignty. The occupation, at the site of the 1890 massacre, draws global media attention to Indigenous rights and government treaty violations. It galvanizes the modern Indigenous rights movement and leads to the 2007 UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples.

title: Standing Rock — Water Is Life
summary: The Sioux and allies block the Dakota Access Pipeline. Indigenous environmental activism goes global.
body: The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe and thousands of supporters establish camps to block construction of the Dakota Access Pipeline, which threatens their water supply and crosses sacred burial grounds. The 'Water Protectors' face militarized police, attack dogs, and water cannons in freezing temperatures. The protest becomes a global symbol of Indigenous-led environmental resistance and the intersection of climate justice with Indigenous sovereignty.
